---
title: "Computer science crash course"
description: "Twenty quests ‚Äî CS principles for R ecologists: types, memory, algorithms, debugging, reproducibility."
date: 2026-07-21
sidebar: ai-curriculum
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-location: left
    toc-depth: 2

  warning: false

## Computer science crash course for ecologists

You do not need a computer science degree to analyse reef counts or tag data ‚Äî but a **thin layer of CS thinking** makes everything else click: why `factor` levels matter, why joins duplicate rows, why a loop is slow on a million detections, why your script works on your laptop but not on a collaborator‚Äôs.

These quests use the same rhythm as the functional programming tracks (~10 minutes, annotated R where it helps, reef / fishery / acoustic / biologging examples). The goal is **competence**: read code, write code, debug code ‚Äî whether you type it yourself or use a tool later.



## Q1 ¬∑ Programs, data, and R {#q01}

A **program** is instructions that transform **input data** into **output** (tables, plots, model fits). R is both a calculator and a language for chaining those transformations ‚Äî your transect CSV is input; a species richness table is output.

Thinking in input ‚Üí process ‚Üí output matches how you design a field study: samples in, inference out.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
input_rows <- nrow(reef)                              # how many rows enter the pipeline
output_sites <- length(unique(reef$site))               # how many sites after summarising
c(input_rows = input_rows, output_sites = output_sites) # named result vector
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
  input_rows output_sites 
          60            3 
```


:::
:::


## Q2 ¬∑ Assignment and names {#q02}

**`<-`** stores a value under a **name** (object). Names should describe meaning (`mean_depth`), not typing order (`x1`). The environment is a lookup table from names to values ‚Äî the same idea as labelled columns, but for interim results.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
mean_depth <- mean(reef$depth_m)                      # store summary under a clear name
mean_depth                                                # retrieve by name
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
[1] 7.591667
```


:::

```{.r .cell-code}
rm(mean_depth)                                            # remove binding (free name)
```
:::


## Q3 ¬∑ Types and coercion {#q03}

Every value has a **type** (`numeric`, `character`, `logical`, ‚Ä¶). **Coercion** converts types, sometimes silently ‚Äî `"12"` + 1 fails; `as.numeric("12")` succeeds. Ecology CSVs often import counts as character if a stray `"NA"` or `"."` appears.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
typeof(reef$count)                                      # should be double/integer
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
[1] "integer"
```


:::

```{.r .cell-code}
char_counts <- as.character(reef$count[1:3])              # simulate import as text
as.numeric(char_counts)                                   # explicit conversion back
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
[1]  4 12  1
```


:::
:::


## Q4 ¬∑ Memory: copy vs modify {#q04}

R often **shares** memory until you **modify** a copy (copy-on-write). Large acoustic tables feel slow when you copy them repeatedly in a loop. Prefer one pipeline that adds columns once, or tools (**data.table** `:=`) that modify in place when you mean to.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
a <- reef$count                                         # share column vector
b <- a                                                    # b points at same data until changed
b[1] <- 999L                                              # replace triggers copy for b
a[1]                                                      # original column unchanged in reef
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
[1] 4
```


:::
:::


## Q5 ¬∑ Files and paths {#q05}

Scripts live in **directories**; data live in **files** with **paths**. **`getwd()`** is where R thinks you are; **`file.path()`** builds paths portably. Reproducible projects use relative paths from a project root ‚Äî not `setwd("~/Desktop")` on one machine only.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
getwd()                                                 # current working directory
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
[1] "/Users/owuss/Documents/GitHub/website-building/ai-tutorials/cs-crash-course"
```


:::

```{.r .cell-code}
file.path("ai-tutorials", "data", "reef_transects.csv") # portable path string
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
[1] "ai-tutorials/data/reef_transects.csv"
```


:::

```{.r .cell-code}
file.exists(data_path("reef_transects.csv"))              # TRUE when CSV is found
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
[1] TRUE
```


:::
:::


## Q6 ¬∑ Functions as contracts {#q06}

A **function** is a contract: **inputs** (arguments), **output** (return value), and **side effects** (files written, messages). Good contracts are small and testable ‚Äî one function for ‚ÄúCPUE per trip‚Äù, not one script that does everything.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
cpue <- function(catch, effort) {                     # catch / effort with guard
  if (effort <= 0) return(NA_real_)                     # invalid effort ‚Üí missing
  catch / effort
}
cpue(catch = 40, effort = 8)                              # example call
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
[1] 5
```


:::
:::


## Q7 ¬∑ Boolean logic {#q07}

**Logical** values (`TRUE` / `FALSE`) drive **`if`**, **`filter()`**, and indexing. **`&`** is element-wise AND; **`&&`** is scalar AND (first pair only). **`any()`** / **`all()`** summarise vectors ‚Äî ‚Äúany depth negative?‚Äù, ‚Äúall tags unique?‚Äù.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
bad_depth <- reef$depth_m < 0                           # logical vector
any(bad_depth, na.rm = TRUE)                              # any impossible depths?
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
[1] FALSE
```


:::

```{.r .cell-code}
all(!duplicated(acoustic$animal_id) | TRUE)               # tags can repeat rows; logic demo
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
[1] TRUE
```


:::

```{.r .cell-code}
table(acoustic$detections > 3)                              # TRUE/FALSE counts
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```

FALSE  TRUE 
   12    12 
```


:::
:::


## Q8 ¬∑ Algorithms: find and sort {#q08}

An **algorithm** is a step-by-step recipe. **`which.max()`** finds an index; **`order()`** returns sort indices. For small ecology tables, ‚Äúfast enough‚Äù beats clever ‚Äî for million-row detections, algorithm choice matters (Quest 9).


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
i_max <- which.max(fishery$tonnes)                      # row with largest landing
fishery[i_max, c("year", "species", "tonnes")]            # inspect that record
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
  year species tonnes
9 2020 Snapper  144.5
```


:::

```{.r .cell-code}
order(fishery$tonnes, decreasing = TRUE)[1:3]             # top three indices
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
[1]  9 13 12
```


:::
:::


## Q9 ¬∑ Big-O intuition for n rows {#q09}

**Big-O** describes how work grows with **n** (rows). Doubling rows roughly doubles time for a single **`O(n)`** pass; nested loops over sites √ó species can be **`O(n¬≤)`** on wide problems. Vectorised R is often **`O(n)`** in C ‚Äî loops in pure R are fine for teaching, costly at IMOS scale.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
n <- nrow(acoustic)                                     # n for this file
c(rows = n, double_pass = 2 * n, nested_demo = length(unique(acoustic$animal_id)) * 10)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
       rows double_pass nested_demo 
         24          48          30 
```


:::
:::


## Q10 ¬∑ Vectors, lists, tables {#q10}

**Vector** = one type, one dimension. **List** = mixed objects. **Data frame** = table of columns (vectors of equal length). Ecology exports are tables; nested receiver lists are lists ‚Äî know which you hold.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
str(reef$count)                                         # atomic vector
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
 int [1:60] 4 12 1 7 21 6 7 2 9 20 ...
```


:::

```{.r .cell-code}
str(split(reef$count, reef$site))                       # list of vectors
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
List of 3
 $ East : int [1:20] 6 13 4 5 26 11 10 8 8 26 ...
 $ North: int [1:20] 4 12 1 7 21 6 8 1 2 17 ...
 $ South: int [1:20] 6 7 2 9 20 15 10 0 4 22 ...
```


:::

```{.r .cell-code}
str(reef)                                                 # data.frame of columns
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
'data.frame':	60 obs. of  5 variables:
 $ transect_id: chr  "T01" "T01" "T01" "T01" ...
 $ site       : chr  "North" "North" "North" "North" ...
 $ species    : chr  "Parrotfish" "Surgeonfish" "Grouper" "Snapper" ...
 $ count      : int  4 12 1 7 21 6 7 2 9 20 ...
 $ depth_m    : num  6.2 6.2 6.2 6.2 6.2 5.2 5.2 5.2 5.2 5.2 ...
```


:::
:::


## Q11 ¬∑ Encoding and factors {#q11}

**Factors** store **levels** (categories) efficiently; **character** stores full strings. Stats models often want factors; joins want consistent spelling. **`forcats`** helps in tidy workflows; base **`as.factor()`** is enough to understand the idea.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
f <- as.factor(reef$site)                               # site as categorical
levels(f)                                                 # allowed labels
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
[1] "East"  "North" "South"
```


:::

```{.r .cell-code}
as.integer(f)[1:5]                                        # internal level codes
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
[1] 2 2 2 2 2
```


:::
:::


## Q12 ¬∑ Reproducibility & seeds {#q12}

**Reproducibility** means someone else (or future you) gets the same result from the same code and data. **`set.seed()`** fixes pseudo-random draws for subsampling and simulation in papers and supplements.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
set.seed(42)                                            # lock RNG
sample(reef$transect_id, 3)                               # three random transects
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
[1] "T10" "T08" "T01"
```


:::

```{.r .cell-code}
set.seed(42)                                              # same seed again
sample(reef$transect_id, 3)                               # identical draw
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
[1] "T10" "T08" "T01"
```


:::
:::


## Q13 ¬∑ Debugging strategies {#q13}

**Debugging** is hypothesis testing: predict, inspect, narrow. **`str()`**, **`head()`**, **`nrow()`**, **`summary()`**, and **`browser()`** (when needed) beat randomly changing code. Halve the pipeline until the bug appears ‚Äî same as ruling out gear failure on a boat.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
str(acoustic)                                           # structure first
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
'data.frame':	24 obs. of  4 variables:
 $ animal_id : chr  "A101" "A101" "A101" "A101" ...
 $ receiver  : chr  "R11" "R2" "R6" "R1" ...
 $ detections: int  5 2 7 4 5 4 4 1 3 3 ...
 $ reef_zone : chr  "Slope" "Crest" "Slope" "Crest" ...
```


:::

```{.r .cell-code}
summary(acoustic$detections)                              # range and NA check
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
   Min. 1st Qu.  Median    Mean 3rd Qu.    Max. 
  1.000   2.000   3.500   3.792   5.000   7.000 
```


:::

```{.r .cell-code}
stopifnot(nrow(acoustic) > 0)                             # assert expectation early
```
:::


## Q14 ¬∑ Version control mindset {#q14}

**Version control** (Git) tracks **changes** to scripts over time ‚Äî who changed what, when, and why. You do not need every Git command on day one; you need the habit: **small commits**, meaningful messages, one analysis per branch. Field notebooks have dates; code should too.

*(This quest is conceptual ‚Äî no Git commands required in R.)*

## Q15 ¬∑ DRY and small functions {#q15}

**DRY** (Don‚Äôt Repeat Yourself): one definition, many uses. Copy-pasted **`mutate()`** blocks drift apart; a function or shared script chunk stays consistent across reef and fishery reports.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
z_score <- function(x) (x - mean(x, na.rm = TRUE)) / sd(x, na.rm = TRUE)  # reusable
z_score(reef$count)[1:5]
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
[1] -0.7841578  0.2881947 -1.1862899 -0.3820256  1.4945913
```


:::

```{.r .cell-code}
z_score(fishery$tonnes)[1:5]
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
[1]  0.51776773  0.64938929 -1.24393627  0.24777478 -0.07959167
```


:::
:::


## Q16 ¬∑ Validate inputs early {#q16}

**Fail fast**: check column names, types, and ranges at the top of a script. Clear **`stop()`** messages save hours when a CSV column was renamed (`Detections` vs `detections`).


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
need <- c("animal_id", "detections")                   # required columns
if (!all(need %in% names(acoustic))) stop("Missing columns: ", paste(setdiff(need, names(acoustic)), collapse = ", "))
if (any(acoustic$detections < 0, na.rm = TRUE)) stop("Negative detections found")
message("Input checks passed")
```
:::


## Q17 ¬∑ Composition and pipes {#q17}

**Composition** chains small steps: output of step 1 feeds step 2. The **pipe** **`|>`** passes the left object as the first argument to the right ‚Äî readable top-to-bottom narratives for dplyr pipelines.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
suppressPackageStartupMessages(library(dplyr))
reef |>
  filter(count > 0) |>
  group_by(site) |>
  summarise(total = sum(count), .groups = "drop")
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
# A tibble: 3 √ó 2
  site  total
  <chr> <int>
1 East    219
2 North   176
3 South   196
```


:::
:::


## Q18 ¬∑ Reading error messages {#q18}

Errors read **bottom-up**: what failed, then the call stack. **`object not found`** ‚Üí typo in a name. **`non-numeric argument`** ‚Üí wrong type. Save the full message when asking for help ‚Äî it is data.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
# Example: intentional typo (run to see error in console when knitting locally)
# mean(reef$counts)  # counts vs count
tryCatch(mean(reef$counts), error = function(e) conditionMessage(e))
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
[1] NA
```


:::
:::


## Q19 ¬∑ Documentation & help() {#q19}

**`?mean`**, **`help(mean)`**, and package vignettes are authoritative. **`args(mean)`** shows the argument list quickly. Writing a one-line comment per custom function is documentation your future self will thank you for.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
args(mean)                                              # formal arguments
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
function (x, ...) 
NULL
```


:::

```{.r .cell-code}
?mean                                                     # opens help (may be suppressed in render)
```
:::


## Q20 ¬∑ CS capstone plan {#q20}

Before writing code for a new dataset, sketch on paper:

1. **Inputs** ‚Äî files, columns, units  
2. **Outputs** ‚Äî tables, figures, model objects  
3. **Checks** ‚Äî row counts, NA rules, keys  
4. **Steps** ‚Äî import ‚Üí clean ‚Üí summarise ‚Üí plot  

That plan is what good coursework, theses, and later **tool-assisted** coding all depend on ‚Äî the tool fills syntax; you own the design.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
plan <- list(
  input = "acoustic_detections.csv",
  output = "detections per animal_id",
  checks = c("no negative detections", "animal_id not empty"),
  steps = c("read.csv", "group_by + summarise", "ggplot if needed")
)
plan
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
$input
[1] "acoustic_detections.csv"

$output
[1] "detections per animal_id"

$checks
[1] "no negative detections" "animal_id not empty"   

$steps
[1] "read.csv"             "group_by + summarise" "ggplot if needed"    
```


:::
:::
